The first set puts many small parts together by parts that come from the way their inside parts are built; the second way puts living things together by the way their bodies look, which shows they share a same family; the rock way uses the way the tiny parts are built, which decides how hard they are, how they break, and how they let light go through.  

From these big case we can try to make a way to put things into groups that is good for study only when it puts together things that share big important parts, when the groups stay the same when we see new things, and when the whole way fits with the best ideas we have.  

But this way, even though it works for the big case, also lets in ways to put things together that are not good for study.  

Think about a way to put stars into groups by how bright they look from our world — how much light we see.  

Stars that look about the same bright share a part that matters when we look at them; new stars we find can be given a bright look number without changing the groups; and this way of grouping fits well with the ideas we have about space, because the bright look number is a clear thing we can see.  

All three parts of the way are true.  

But this way of grouping does not tell us much about the stars, because it puts together stars that are really very different — a small close star that is not bright and a huge far star that is very bright can look the same bright — and it puts together stars that are really the same.  

The part we use to make the groups — how bright they look — is about where we are looking from, not about any real part of the stars.  

The big case worked because it used parts that belong to the things themselves, not because of where we look at them.  

The way says the parts we group by must be important, but it does not say they must be parts that belong to the things themselves and not to the way we see them.
definition: A system of classification C is scientifically adequate if and only if (i) C groups objects that share important properties, (ii) the groupings in C are stable under new observations — that is, newly discovered objects can be assigned to existing categories without restructuring the system, and (iii) C is consistent with the best available scientific theory.
counterexample: Consider a classification of stars based on their apparent brightness as seen from Earth — grouping together all stars of similar apparent magnitude. Stars of similar apparent brightness do share an 'important' property (they appear equally bright to an observer on Earth), the groupings are stable under new observations (newly discovered stars can readily be assigned an apparent magnitude), and the classification is consistent with astronomical theory (apparent magnitude is a well-defined observational quantity). All three conditions are met. Yet the classification groups together stars that are intrinsically very different — a dim nearby star and a luminous distant star may have the same apparent magnitude — and separates stars that are intrinsically similar. The classification is scientifically inadequate because apparent brightness reflects the observer's position rather than any property of the stars themselves.

question: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
expected_answer:
One might think that a group of numbers that you can add and see which is bigger in order is whole if and only if it has the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number, it has all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number inside it, and every number in the group can be written as one whole number over another whole number.  

The first two ideas make sense: the real numbers have the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number, and they do have all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number inside them.  

The third idea might seem to make sense if what you know about these groups of numbers is only the ones that can be written as one whole number over another whole number. Every such number is, by the words that say what it is, a part of one whole number over another whole number, and that group of numbers follows all three ideas.  

Yet one must think about the real numbers – the group that most people think of as the main whole group of numbers. The real numbers have the idea that you can always find a whole number bigger than any number: for any number there is a whole number that is bigger. They have all the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number.  

But not every real number is a part of one whole number over another whole number. The number that when you put it together with itself gives the number 2 is not a part of whole numbers, a fact that people have shown a long time ago and is one of the first and biggest things people have found in numbers. It cannot be written as one whole number over another whole number, no matter how in a good way you try to pick the top and bottom whole numbers.  

The third idea of the words that say what it is does not work for the real numbers.  

But the real numbers are whole; in fact, having the idea that any group of real numbers that has at least one number and has a biggest number also has a smallest biggest number inside the group is what makes them whole.  

The words that say what the numbers are only talked about the numbers that can be written as one whole number over another whole number, not about the idea that makes a group whole, and so they put together groups that do not have the idea with groups that do.
definition: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
counterexample: Consider the real numbers. The real numbers form an ordered field that is Archimedean and contains the rational numbers as a subfield. Yet not every real number can be expressed as a ratio of two integers; the square root of 2, for instance, is irrational — a fact established by the ancient Greeks. The third condition fails, yet the field of real numbers is the paradigmatic example of a complete ordered field. The definition has confused completeness with the property of being the rational field.
missing_condition: The definition requires that every element be a ratio of integers, which characterises the rational field, not a complete ordered field. What is wanted is the condition that every non-empty subset with an upper bound has a least upper bound — the supremum property — which is the standard characterisation of completeness for ordered fields.

question: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
expected_answer: One might call a good talk about a big good thing as a talk that starts from thoughts that only reason can see, uses right steps and ends with the thought that a thing that has all the best things is real. This name shows the before any look step by step important part that we join with the most well known talks about the big good thing that come from looking at the world. Yet a case that shows this not enough can be taken from the old way of thinking about a thing. Imagine a person who thinks makes a talk that starts from the thought that a thing is a best thing and the thought that we can think of a thing that has all the best things. By right steps the person ends with the thought that a thing that has all the best things is real. The thoughts are shown as things that only reason can see, the steps are right and the end is as we need. All three parts of the name we gave are met. Still one might see that the good talk is made from the thing we take as true that a thing is a real part that can be put with the best parts of a thing. If a thing is not a real part — if to say a thing is real does not add a new part to it but only says that a thought is there — then the thought that a thing is a best thing is not known to be true by reason alone, even if it looks clear at first. It looks like the name, by asking only that thoughts be known by reason alone, does not ask that this reason be real instead of just looks like, and so it can not keep out talks that rest on things many people talk about or that starts with a thing that already says the answer.
definition: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
counterexample: Suppose a philosopher constructs an argument from the premise that existence is a perfection and the premise that we can conceive of a being possessing all perfections, proceeding by valid deductive steps to the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists. The premises are presented as knowable by reason alone, the inferences are valid, and the conclusion is as stated. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the argument depends upon treating existence as a property that can be included among the perfections of a being, an assumption that is by no means self-evident. If existence is not a genuine predicate — if saying that something exists is not attributing a property to it in the same way as saying it is wise or powerful — then the premise that existence is a perfection is not known to be true by reason alone, despite appearing to be so.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the premises be genuinely self-evident or demonstrably true, rather than merely appearing to be knowable by reason alone, so that an argument resting upon a contested or question-begging premise is not counted as sound merely because the premise has the superficial character of a rational truth.

question: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
expected_answer:
One might say that a thing is a whole made of parts only when each part stays inside the whole, the parts together fill the whole with no empty places left, and the parts do not cover the same space.  

This idea makes a clear look: a whole is like a look made of pieces that fit together with no empty places and no parts that share the same space.  

The idea that each part stays inside makes sure no part goes outside the whole.  

The idea that the parts together fill the whole makes sure the whole is completely covered.  

And the idea that parts do not share the same space makes sure each space of the whole belongs to only one part.  

But some may see that this look of parts is too hard for many wholes we see in the real world.  

Think about a human body and its work parts. The blood group and the feeling group are both parts of the body.  

Each stays inside the body.  

Together with the bone group, the moving group, the food group, and other parts, they together fill the body.  

But they share some of the same space: blood lines go through feeling part, small blood lines go into the head and the back bone, and you cannot put one of these parts in a place without the other also being there.  

The idea that parts do not share space does not work here, but no one would say that the blood group and the feeling group are not parts of the body.  

The idea comes from the look of cutting a thing into parts that do not share space, but the real way a living thing is made is into work parts that can go through each other free.  

What we need is a big idea of being a part that lets parts share space when we tell them different by what they do, not by where they are.
definition: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
counterexample: Consider the human body as a whole, and consider its circulatory system and its nervous system as two of its parts. Each system is spatially contained within the boundaries of the body. Together with the other organ systems, they jointly exhaust the body — no region of the body is left unaccounted for. Yet the circulatory system and the nervous system do spatially overlap: blood vessels run alongside and through nervous tissue, and capillaries penetrate the brain, the spinal cord, and peripheral nerves. The third condition is violated. Yet the circulatory system and the nervous system are plainly parts of the body, and the body is plainly a whole composed of such parts. The definition excludes a perfectly legitimate and scientifically standard decomposition of a whole into its functional parts.
missing_condition: The definition requires that parts be spatially non-overlapping, but this is too restrictive for wholes whose natural decomposition is into functional systems rather than spatial regions. A condition permitting overlap when the parts are distinguished by function rather than by spatial exclusivity is needed.

question: counterexample to: A set A is larger than a set B if and only if (i) A contains more elements than B in the sense that A has elements not in B, (ii) B is a proper subset of A, and (iii) A is not in one-to-one correspondence with B.
expected_answer:
A big change in the idea about never ending groups showed that the feeling idea of “bigger” needs to be changed when it is used on never ending groups. The counting numbers hold all the counting numbers and more, yet a way to put each thing in one group with exactly one thing in the other group puts them in the same size. The numbers that are parts of whole numbers are close together in the space between any two counting numbers, yet a special way of looking at a group of numbers written in order makes a new number that is different from each number in the group and shows they also have the same size as the counting numbers. Only the never ending numbers get away from this way of putting each thing with exactly one thing. The special way of looking at a group of numbers written in order shows that any group of the never ending numbers can not hold them all.

Someone might try to say that one group is bigger than another group if the first group has some things that are not in the second, the second group is a group that is inside the first and not the same, and the first group can not be put each thing with exactly one thing in the second. The first two ideas fit the feeling idea that a bigger group has everything the smaller group has, and also more. But a case that shows the idea is not enough shows up right away with the counting numbers and the numbers that can be made by taking a counting number and making it two times bigger. The numbers that can be made by taking a counting number and making it two times bigger are a group that is inside the counting numbers, and the counting numbers have things – the numbers that are not even – that are not in the group of even numbers. Yet the counting numbers and the even numbers can be put each thing with exactly one thing by the way that changes each counting number into the number that is two times bigger. The first two ideas work but the third does not, so the idea in a right way does not say the counting numbers are bigger.

But the idea would not work for a case where one group is bigger but is not a group that holds all the things of the other – a case with the never ending numbers and the counting numbers, where the inside group idea does not matter. What the other cases show is that the size of a group is decided only by whether a way of putting each thing with exactly one thing can be made. The inside group idea is a thing that seems important but is not needed or enough for not the same size. The idea, by using the inside group idea as a way, puts together two ideas that do not need each other.
definition: A set A is larger than a set B if and only if (i) A contains more elements than B in the sense that A has elements not in B, (ii) B is a proper subset of A, and (iii) A is not in one-to-one correspondence with B.
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers N = {1, 2, 3, ...} and the set of even natural numbers E = {2, 4, 6, ...}. E is a proper subset of N, and N contains elements not in E (namely the odd numbers). Conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied. Yet Cantor's bijection n -> 2n places N in one-to-one correspondence with E, so condition (iii) fails. The definition would therefore deny that N is larger than E, which accords with the Cantorian verdict. But now consider the same definition applied to N and the real numbers R: R contains elements not in N, and one might argue that N is a subset of R. Yet R is not in one-to-one correspondence with N by Cantor's theorem. The definition gives the right answer here but for the wrong reason — it conflates the proper-subset relation with the cardinality relation.

question: counterexample to: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.
expected_answer:
The big changes that many people call true big changes all have one thing that makes us want to look at them. The first big change in a country that started the idea that people have a right and that the people should decide was driven by the belief that people have a right and that the people should be the ones who decide. The second big change in a country that made clear ideas about the group that makes laws and about people being able to believe was shaped by those clear ideas. The third big change in a country that followed a clear belief in a right that comes from being a person and a shared idea that people agree to live together was led by that clear belief. In each case the big change was not just a take over of power but a change led by ideas about how the group that makes laws and the way people live should be set up.  

One could try to say that a big change is a quick take over of the old way a country runs that makes a new way that is very different. But a case that shows this short idea is not enough can be taken from the past of quick take overs. Imagine the leader of the fighters takes control in one week, breaks up the group that makes laws, stops the big set of laws for the country, and makes a way of deciding where the fighters are the only ones who decide. The old way is taken away. The change is quick. The new way – a way where one strong person who fights is the only one who decides – is very different from the old way where many people made laws together. All three parts are there. Yet some people see that this quick take over is not a big change. It does not have the belief part that made the first, second, and third big changes. The leader of the fighters has no plan for how people should live together, no clear idea of what is right, no want beyond making his own power stronger. What the past cases have together – and what the short idea does not ask for – is that the big change is led by a clear belief that moves the people and makes the new way they build.
definition: An event E constitutes a genuine revolution if and only if (i) E involves the overthrow of an existing political order, (ii) E occurs rapidly relative to the normal pace of political change, and (iii) E results in a new political order that is qualitatively different from its predecessor.
counterexample: Consider a military coup in which a general seizes power, dissolves the parliament, and establishes a military dictatorship within the space of a week. The existing political order is overthrown. The change occurs rapidly. The new order — military dictatorship — is qualitatively different from the parliamentary system it replaced. All three conditions are met. Yet a military coup is not ordinarily regarded as a revolution. It is a seizure of power by an individual or faction, unaccompanied by the ideological framework, the popular mobilisation, or the aspiration to transform social relations that characterise genuine revolutions.

question: counterexample to: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object in relevant respects, competent viewers recognise the object in the painting, and the painter intended to depict that object.
expected_answer:
If a person looks at many pictures that clearly show what they are about — a picture of a rich man, a picture of a place with land and water, a picture of food that grows on a living thing — that person may think that a picture needs to look like the thing, that a person who looks can see the thing, and that the person who paints meant to show the thing. In each case, the shapes on the paper are the same as the parts of the thing that can be seen, a person who looks can see the thing easy, and the person who paints tried to show it. The big idea that this is the clear idea for what a picture is comes easy: these three parts hold what the cases have the same.

But the big idea is too small when we try to use it on other pictures. Imagine a person sees a picture of a sound thing with thin lines that make sound that is made in a kind that breaks the thing into many flat shapes. The paper shows many flat shapes that cover each other and show many views at the same time, and it does not look like a sound thing in the simple way we usually see a sound thing. A person who looks can not see the usual shape, the dark color of a tree, or the thin lines that make sound just by looking. The first part of the big idea is not met. Still, a person who looks who knows the usual way of this kind can see a sound thing in the picture. They know that the broken look is a way to show a thing, not a problem that stops the picture from showing it. The person who paints also meant to show a sound thing. The second and third parts are met. So the picture can be a picture of a sound thing in a good way. This makes us see that the idea we made from pictures that look like real things was wrong to think that looking like the thing is always needed. A picture can show a thing by using a usual way, by making a shape, and by learned power, even when it does not look like the thing in the way the idea says.
definition: A painting represents an object if and only if it visually resembles that object in relevant respects, competent viewers recognise the object in the painting, and the painter intended to depict that object.
counterexample: Suppose a painter in the cubist tradition produces a painting of a guitar. The canvas presents fragmented geometric planes, overlapping facets, and multiple simultaneous perspectives. The painting does not visually resemble a guitar in any straightforward sense — one cannot identify the familiar shape, colour, or texture of a guitar by simple visual comparison. Yet competent viewers, acquainted with the conventions of cubism, recognise a guitar in the painting, and the painter intended to depict one. The first condition fails, but the painting nonetheless represents a guitar. The inductive inference from portraits, landscapes, and still lifes — all executed in broadly realist idioms — has yielded a definition too closely tied to visual resemblance.

question: counterexample to: An inference from particular instances to a general conclusion is inductively strong if and only if (i) the observed instances are numerous, (ii) the observed instances are varied in their circumstances, and (iii) no observed instance contradicts the general conclusion.
expected_answer: One might call a strong guess that comes from many different and not wrong things we have seen. This call has a feeling of power: we feel more sure in a big idea when it has been shown many times, in many different things, with no one saying it wrong. The points seem to keep from both not enough showing and pick only some things. Yet a case that shows these points are not enough can come from looking at birds. Imagine a person who looks at big white birds in many places in the land we are in over many years. She sees them in many places, in cold time of year and hot time of year, on water and on moving water and near land. Her count goes to many many. Every bird she sees is white. The many things we have seen are many, so they meet the first point. They are different in time of year, in place where they live, and in where they are, so they meet the second point. And no one shows it wrong, so they meet the third point. The guess that “All big white birds are white” looks, by this point, as strong as can be. But the big idea is not true: big white birds in a far away land are black. The many different things in the person look, even though many, do not reach the part that matters – places far away. One can see that the point, by only saying that things must be different but not saying how they must be different, lets a look be very different in ways that do not matter while staying the same in the way that would show the big idea is wrong. The look of strong guess is a thing that looks like it is real but is not, because it only looks in a small area. What the point does not have is a need that the different things be about the things that might make the big idea wrong.
definition: An inference from particular instances to a general conclusion is inductively strong if and only if (i) the observed instances are numerous, (ii) the observed instances are varied in their circumstances, and (iii) no observed instance contradicts the general conclusion.
counterexample: Consider a scientist who has observed ten thousand swans, all in different regions of Europe, at different seasons, in different habitats — lakes, rivers, estuaries, and marshes. All are white. The instances are numerous, varied, and none contradicts the conclusion that all swans are white. All three conditions are satisfied, and the induction appears maximally strong. Yet the conclusion is false, for there exist black swans in Australia. The conditions fail to ensure inductive strength because they take no account of whether the variation in circumstances extends to the relevant variable — in this case, geographical region — that would expose the generalisation's limits.
missing_condition: The definition fails to require that the variation in observed instances be relevant variation — that it extend to those dimensions along which exceptions to the generalisation, if any exist, would be found.

question: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.
expected_answer:
The most long past stories share a part that needs close look. When a person who wrote a long time ago told the fall of the big city that was the main place of an old group, he added the points of look of people who ran the city, outside war people, and people who thought about the one many people think about, showing how their different thoughts made the way things went. When a person who wrote a long time ago told the wars between the group from the east and the group from the west, he wrote down the points of look of both groups, making his story more full by showing that the same things meant different meaning for different people. When a person who wrote a long time ago told the big change in a far away land, he gave a say to country workers and city poor together with the people who led the group, showing that the meaning of the change came from the point of look of the one who lived it.

One could try to make a way to say what a good past story is by saying a good past story is one that tells things in the right order of time, uses real first hand things, and gives a clear why and how story. But a case that shows this way is not enough can be taken from the past writing about the church war. Think of a story made only from west church people – letters from the head of the church, notes from people who lived in a church house, papers from groups that fought in the war. It tells things in the right order. Its things are very real. It gives a clear why the church war started and how it went. All three parts are met. But the story leaves out all the points of look of the people who believed in a different one many people think about whose lands were taken, the people who believed in a group of ideas about the one many people think about that were killed on the way, and the big land in the east whose wants were often put below those of the people from the west who fought in the war. Its clear view is got by a simple leaving out.

One could see that what big stories share, and what the way does not ask for, is the putting in many points of look – the idea that past things are lived and thought about in different ways by different people, and that a story that leaves these points out gets a wrong clear view.
definition: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.
counterexample: Consider a narrative of the Crusades composed entirely from Western Christian sources. It recounts events in correct temporal sequence, draws upon authentic charters, chronicles, and papal correspondence, and provides a coherent account of why the Crusades were launched and how they unfolded. All three conditions are met. Yet the narrative entirely omits the perspectives of the Muslim, Jewish, and Byzantine populations who experienced the Crusades from the other side. The coherence of the account is achieved by the systematic exclusion of perspectives that would complicate and in some cases contradict the narrative's causal thread.

question: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
expected_answer:
One can say a thing that is a kind of stuff that cannot be broken by normal ways, whose tiny parts all have the same number of tiny push parts, and that is found on the ground we live on.  

This way of talking about it holds the old group of things like the light stuff we breathe, the light stuff we breathe, the bright metal, the strong metal, with true.  

But a story that goes against the third thing we check can be taken from the past of big small part study.  

Think about the thing with the number 43.  

It cannot be broken into simpler stuff by normal ways.  

All of its tiny parts have 43 push parts.  

But it is not found on the ground we live on in any big enough size.  

The thing with number 43 was first made in 1937 by hitting the thing with number 42 with fast tiny parts in a big machine that makes fast tiny parts.  

Its life on the ground we live on is all made by people.  

The third thing we check is broken.  

But the thing with number 43 is for sure a thing that is a kind of stuff.  

It sits in its spot in the big group of things with numbers between the thing with number 42 and the thing with number 44, makes together parts that can be guessed, and acts in every way the thing with number 43 should act.  

It seems that the way we say it, by saying it must be found on the ground we live on, adds a place stop into what is really an idea of simple stuff.  

A thing is said by its number, not by where or how it comes to be found.
definition: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
counterexample: Consider the element technetium, atomic number 43. It cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, and all of its atoms have 43 protons. But technetium does not occur naturally on Earth in any appreciable quantity — it was first produced artificially in a cyclotron. The third condition is violated. Yet technetium is unquestionably a chemical element; it occupies its proper place in the periodic table and exhibits all the chemical properties one would expect of element 43.
missing_condition: The definition restricts elements to naturally occurring substances on Earth and thereby fails to accommodate synthetic elements, which are no less genuinely elements for being produced in laboratories rather than found in nature.

question: counterexample to: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainted — that is, P has direct knowledge of Q's character gained through repeated personal interaction, (ii) P has observed Q acting reliably in the past and infers that Q will act reliably in the future, and (iii) the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P.
expected_answer:
Think about three things where trust is simple. A man gives money to his neighbor, trusting the money will be paid back. His trust comes from knowing each other; he has known the neighbor for many years, has seen him when they work together, and has found him always on time with what he must do. A person who sells things lets a long time buyer take things now, trusting the buyer is good, which he has had many chances to see over many years of buying and selling. A person who grows food puts his work things in an open small building, trusting that his neighbors — men he has known since he was a child — will take care of his things. From these things you could make a simple saying: a person trusts another if and only if they know each other by meeting many times, the first has seen the second act in a good way before, and the trust goes both ways.

But we also need to think about the trust that a person who rides in a big flying thing puts in the person who flies the big flying thing. The person who rides comes to the place where big flying things start, shows his paper that lets him go, walks through the door where people get on, sits down, puts the thing that holds his pants on, and gives his life — with no worry and no fear — to the person who will fly the big flying thing. Yet the person who rides has never met the person who flies. He does not know the name of the person who flies, has no knowing of the ways of the person who flies, and has never seen the person who flies do any act, good or bad. His trust does not come from knowing a person or seeing before; it comes from something else — from the things that tell how to act for people who fly big things, from the fact that the person who flies has been taught, looked at, and given the yes by a group that makes the things that tell how to act, from the fact that the big flying thing has been looked at and said good, and from the fact that the group that runs the trips must follow the things that tell how to act for being safe made by law. The trust does not go both ways in any real way; the person who flies does not give the safe of the person who rides, and the way they are joined is not the same for both. All three parts of the simple saying do not hold. Yet the trust is real and very strong — as strong as any trust between neighbors, because it is a trust where the life of the person is in a chance of a bad thing.

The earlier things were taken from places where people meet each other, where trust is about a person, seen by watching, and goes both ways, and the simple saying took these parts as important. The big flying thing shows that the trust that holds up new life together is mostly not about a person, is made by groups, and is one way, and it does not rest on knowing the ways of a person but on the good work of many parts, looks, and the things that tell how to act that the person who trusts may never have looked at and could not decide.
definition: A person P trusts a person Q if and only if (i) P and Q are personally acquainted — that is, P has direct knowledge of Q's character gained through repeated personal interaction, (ii) P has observed Q acting reliably in the past and infers that Q will act reliably in the future, and (iii) the trust is reciprocal — Q also trusts P.
counterexample: Consider the trust that a passenger places in the pilot of an aeroplane. The passenger boards the aircraft, entrusts his life to the pilot, and sits calmly throughout the flight in the confidence that the pilot will convey him safely to his destination. Yet the passenger is not personally acquainted with the pilot; he has never met the pilot, does not know the pilot's name, and has no direct knowledge of the pilot's character. He has not observed the pilot acting reliably in the past; he has never seen the pilot fly. And the trust is not reciprocal; the pilot does not trust the passenger in any comparable sense — the pilot does not entrust his safety to the passenger. All three conditions fail, yet the passenger trusts the pilot.

question: counterexample to: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
expected_answer:
The main cases of just control that come from clear yes of the people who live under it. In a town meeting in the new part of the country, people came together to talk and to say yes to the things that tell them how to live. The early written paper was a note that people signed by their own will, making them follow the same set of things that tell people how to live. The big meeting in 1787 made a set of things that people talked about, wrote down, and all said yes to by people chosen to speak for the people. In each other case, the people group got its control from the real clear yes of the people who would live under it.

Someone could try to write down these ideas by saying a just people group thing is one that gets its control from the yes of the people and that lets people stop saying yes when the thing does not help what they want. This idea shows the town meeting, the paper, and the big meeting. But a case that shows it is not enough comes when we think about a kind thing from another land that was taken by strong hand and never said yes by the people, but that gives no war, just law, good things, and safe life. The people have no way to stop saying yes, because they never said yes. All three parts of the idea do not work. Yet imagine the people, asked in a very true way, say they feel good with the thing and do not want it to change.

Someone could see that the thing gives real and liked good things, and that to say it is not just at all seems to not see the good of the very people whose wants the idea says it will keep safe. The other cases, put together with the other land case, show a different in the idea of just control. The idea shows just by yes but cannot fit just by good — and the other land case says the two may be not together. One thing may be said yes to without giving good things to the people, and it may give good things to the people without being said yes to. The idea, by making yes the only place for just, cannot fit this different.
definition: A political arrangement P is legitimate if and only if (i) P derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) the consent is given freely and without coercion, and (iii) the governed have a genuine opportunity to withdraw their consent if the arrangement fails to serve their interests.
counterexample: Consider a just and benevolent colonial administration that provides peace, security, impartial justice, and material prosperity to a colonised population. The population has not consented to the arrangement — it was imposed by conquest. There is no mechanism for withdrawing consent, for consent was never given. All three conditions fail. Yet suppose the population, surveyed honestly, expresses overwhelming satisfaction with the arrangement and has no desire to change it. One might observe that the arrangement, though lacking consent, provides genuine and appreciated benefits, and that its legitimacy — if it has any — rests on something other than the consent of the governed.

question: counterexample to: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
expected_answer:
The ideas of the person who wrote about new things we have before we see them are seen as one of the most big and most talked about adds to how we know things in the long time of that thing.

Against both the person who says reason is the main way to know, who says that things we have before we see are always just about breaking things down, and the person who says seeing and feeling are the main way to know, who says that any real things we have before we see can not be, the person who wrote about these ideas says that there are thoughts that give new facts and can be known without any seeing or feeling.

The things he shows — 7 and 5 make 12, every thing that happens has a reason, and the simple ideas of flat shape work — have been talked about a lot since then.

The meaning we look at now shows the idea of the person who wrote about these ideas by putting the new things we have before we see on the simple ways the mind sees and the main ways the mind sorts.

One may see, however, that this putting is itself a real big idea about thinking, and a thing that many people do not all agree on.

A story that shows the not enough of this look comes up when we think about the view of the person who thinks seeing and feeling are the main way to know and who does not like the things of the big idea of deep thinking by the person who wrote about these ideas.

Such a person may say that some thoughts — every thing that comes after has a reason, for case — look like new things we have before we see, but will say that the before part is not shown by the simple ways the mind sees or the main ways the mind sorts.

She may give another reason using ideas about how the mind gets set, how we get things from our past, or how we need it to work.

Thing number three, which joins the new things we have before we see to a special big idea of deep real things by the person who wrote about these ideas, then makes the meaning take sides where it should be just.

A good story about the new things we have before we see, if those things are real, must fit with many different ways of looking at how we know, instead of thinking that one big idea about thinking is the only real one.
definition: A judgement is synthetic a priori if and only if (i) the predicate is not contained in the concept of the subject, (ii) the judgement is known independently of experience, and (iii) the judgement's truth is grounded in the pure forms of intuition or the categories of the understanding.
counterexample: Consider the judgement 'Every effect has a cause.' One might grant that this is synthetic and a priori, satisfying conditions (i) and (ii). Yet an empiricist may deny that there exist pure forms of intuition or categories of the understanding in Kant's sense, holding instead that the judgement's apparent a priori status is explicable by deeply entrenched habits of thought. Condition (iii) thus depends upon a contested metaphysical framework.
missing_condition: The analysis presupposes the truth of Kant's transcendental idealism and does not provide an account of synthetic a priori knowledge that is neutral between competing epistemological frameworks.

question: counterexample to: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.
expected_answer:
One can look at the way to say a thing in a set of words that follow a set of ways by saying that there is a short group of words that is true for only the things that have the thing and that the thing is only about which things have it and not about how it works inside or maybe other ideas.  

These three ways seem to show many usual cases of the way to say a thing.  

But one can see that they let some cases that do not fit well with some hopes that one may have about the idea.  

Imagine one looks at the thing of being a number that is not a different sign number of a group of words that says something that can be shown true by a certain set of ways.  

This thing is only about which numbers have it.  

Also, the facts from simple counting work tell us that there is a short group of words in the set of words that is true for only those numbers.  

All three ways are true.  

The thing can be said.  

But there is no good way to check, for any number, if it is true for this group of words.  

The thing can be said but cannot be decided by a way.  

If one thought that the way to say a thing in a set of words came with some power — some power, at least in idea, to see cases — then the looking at is shown to be not enough, because it lets the way to say a thing without any such power.  

One can, of course, accept this end and say that the way to say a thing and the way to decide it are just different ideas, but the case at least shows that the looking at does not talk about a part that many people want to see talked about.
definition: A property is definable in a formal language if and only if there exists a formula in that language that is satisfied by exactly those objects possessing the property, the formula is finitely long, and the property is extensional.
counterexample: Suppose one considers the property of being a natural number that is not the Goedel number of a provable sentence of a given formal system. This property is extensional — it depends only on which objects fall under it, not on how they are described. One can construct a finitely long formula that is satisfied by exactly those natural numbers possessing the property (indeed, the representability results of arithmetic show this). All three conditions are met and the property is definable in the formal language. Yet the property in question is not decidable — there is no effective procedure for determining, for an arbitrary number, whether it falls under the formula. One might have supposed that definability in a formal language entails some measure of algorithmic tractability, but the analysis as given does not require this, thereby counting as definable properties that are in principle unrecognisable.
missing_condition: The definition does not address the distinction between definability and decidability, and thus counts as definable certain properties for which no effective method of recognition exists — a point which, depending on one's purposes, may render the analysis insufficient.

question: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
expected_answer: It is often thought that many of our ideas are not always in the head at a time but are like a way of being ready. The look here says that an idea is ready if and only if a person is ready to say yes to the right words when the ask is made in the right way, the ready feeling comes from some earlier thought or way, and the person would really say yes to the words in normal times. A story that shows this look is not enough can be made like this. Think about the words that no big animal is lighter than one small weight. It looks like almost any thinking grown up person would say yes to these words if you ask, and would do it with full truth. But it seems very hard that most people have ever thought about this same words, or that any earlier thought about big animals and small weights has happened in the mind before. The ready feeling to say yes does not come from an earlier thought about making ideas but from the person big idea of the world, which gives the right pick as a simple guess when the time needs it. The look however says that the ready feeling must come from some earlier thought, and this law is not met. So we end up saying that the idea of a ready idea goes past what the look can hold, because it has to have words that the person has never thought about but that follow easy from what the person already knows.
definition: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
counterexample: A person who has never considered the proposition that no elephant weighs less than one gram would, if asked, readily assent to it, and would do so sincerely. Yet the disposition to assent is not grounded in any prior cognitive episode concerning elephants and grams; it arises from general background knowledge that has never been synthesised into that particular proposition.
missing_condition: The analysis neglects the possibility that a disposition to assent may arise from the logical or inferential consequences of existing beliefs without any prior cognitive episode specifically directed at the proposition in question.

question: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
expected_answer: One might say that a way a thing acts is without learning if it is shown by all normal members of the group, shows up without learning, and is done in the same way each time. This idea catches the ways most often called without learning: the making of a thin line by a small many leg thing, the rolling of eggs by gray birds, the drinking of white drink by baby warm blood animal. In each case the way is for all, not learned, and done in the usual, same each time way. Yet a case that shows this idea is not enough is given by song of song birds. The first kind of song is without learning: a young bird kept away from sound will make a simple but still known kind of the group usual song. The song shows up without clear teaching – no bird teaches another to sing the way a person teaches a child to read. It is shown by all normal grown birds. The first thing and the second thing are true. But the song is not done in the same way each time. Young birds must hear grown bird song during a time when they can learn a lot to grow the full kind of song, and each bird makes its own different ways – different sounds in different places, and extra parts that let people who have heard many songs know which bird it is. The third thing is not true. Yet people who study animals still call bird song without learning. One might see that the idea was built from the case of the simple, same each time move – the quick bend of a knee, the quick change of eye. Many of the most important ways without learning in the world have a first kind that is made more clear by what they have lived through, allowing a lot of different ways for each one while staying clearly the usual way for that group.
definition: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
counterexample: Consider birdsong in many passerine species. The basic song template is innate — a young bird raised in isolation will produce a rudimentary version of the species-typical song. It appears without explicit training, and it is universal among normal males of the species. But the song is not performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern. Young birds must hear adult song during a critical period in order to develop the full, species-typical version, and individual birds develop recognisable variations — local dialects and personal flourishes. Condition (iii) fails. Yet birdsong is universally regarded as an instinctive behaviour, albeit one that requires environmental input for its full expression.
missing_condition: The definition requires fixed, stereotyped performance, but many instinctive behaviours — including birdsong, nest-building, and foraging — exhibit individual variation and require environmental input for their full development, without thereby ceasing to be instinctive.

question: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
expected_answer: One might say that a person sees a thing only when the person has a feeling that shows the thing, the person can look at the thing and tell it different from the things around, and the person can name the thing. The words try to cover the case of a clear, known seeing. When I see a red bird sitting on a branch, I have a sight that shows the bird, I can look at it and tell it different from the green leaves, and I can name it – I can say that it is a red bird. Yet we must think about the case of a person walking through a thick and new group of trees. From somewhere on the ground comes a sound – a low, on and off noise that goes up and down with no order. The person stops and listens. He has a feeling that shows the sound; he hears it clear. He can look at it and tell it different from the other sounds of the world – from the wind in the top above, from the far call of a bird, from the sound of a branch moving. But he cannot name it. He does not know what makes the sound. Is it an animal moving through the leaves? Is it the wind shaking the ground? Is it water moving over stones under the leaf floor? He cannot say. He sees the sound – he hears it as clear as he hears his own steps – but he does not know what it is. The last part of the words does not work. The look has asked that seeing also must be able to name, which is more than what seeing alone gives. Seeing gives us feeling of things and things that happen in our world; naming – the putting of what is seen into a name – is a more thinking work that needs what you know, feeling, and ideas that the person might not have. One can see what one cannot name.
definition: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
counterexample: Consider a person walking through a dense forest who hears an unfamiliar sound — a low, intermittent rustling from somewhere in the undergrowth. The person has a sensory experience that represents the sound; he can attend to it and discriminate it from the ambient noise of the wind and the birdsong. Yet he cannot identify it. He cannot say what it is — whether it is an animal, the wind in the leaves, or running water. He perceives the sound; he hears it clearly; but he does not know what it is. The third condition fails, yet the perception is genuine.
missing_condition: The definition requires identification, but perception may occur without the perceiver's being able to identify what they perceive. What is wanted is a recognition that perception is a matter of sensory awareness, not of classificatory knowledge.

question: counterexample to: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.
expected_answer:
One person might say that a thing at one time is the same as a thing at another time only if the two are made of the same stuff parts, they have the same shape, and there is an always time and place way that joins them.  

The idea shows some easy case. A piece of rock taken from a hill and put in a field is, after 20 years, the same piece of rock if it is made of the same stone, keeps the same shape, and has stayed in its place all the time.  

The being the same of simple not changing things seems to sit on just these three things.  

But we have to think about a human. A man at 60 is the same man who at 20 blew out the light on his day sweet food. His friends know him; the law says he must keep promises made many years before; he remembers things from his young days as things that happened to him.  

But the stuff that makes his body has been almost all changed in the 40 years between. The small pieces that made his bones at 20 are not the small pieces that make them at 60; the small parts of his skin, his blood, his inside parts have been made new many times by the usual ways that life keeps them working.  

People who study how bodies work think that in about 7 to 10 years the stuff of a human body is almost all changed.  

The first thing of the idea does not work: the man at 60 is not made of the same stuff parts as the man at 20. But his being the same through time is not in big question.  

The idea has used the staying the same of stuff make up – which is enough for the being the same of not alive things like rocks – and said it is a needed thing for all being the same through time, missing the thing that living things stay the same by changing their stuff parts in an always way of making new and fixing.
definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.
counterexample: Consider the human body. A man at the age of sixty is, by every ordinary reckoning, the same man who at the age of twenty celebrated his birthday. He bears the same name, possesses the same memories, and is recognised by his friends and relations. Yet the material parts of his body have been almost entirely replaced in the intervening forty years. The cells that compose his tissues have been renewed many times over; the atoms that constitute his flesh, his bones, his blood are not the atoms that constituted them four decades earlier. The first condition fails. He is the same man, yet he is not composed of the same material parts.
missing_condition: The definition requires identity of material composition, but living organisms and many artefacts persist through the wholesale replacement of their material parts. What is wanted is an account of identity through time that allows for the gradual replacement of matter provided that some further condition — perhaps continuity of organisation or of causal processes — is satisfied.

question: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
expected_answer: Many people think it is a main good thing about thinking. One might feel like look at it as a very good idea of how far you know, not saying too much, and being ready to say when you are wrong. That way of looking shows a lot of what we mean by not being full of you when we think. But a story that shows this way of looking is not enough and can be made without big trouble. Imagine a person who works with numbers and is very well known who has a very good idea of the edge of what she can do. She knows exactly which parts of number work she can do and which are past what she has learned. She never gives thoughts on things she does not know, and when she makes a wrong thing she says it quickly and does not get angry. She is kind in giving thank for the work of the people she works with. All three parts of the idea seem to be true. But when you look at how she feels more close, you see that she looks at the edge of what she knows with a calm not caring. She does not see them as things that need fixing or as reasons to ask for help from people who know more; instead, she sees them as just facts about her, no more needing a reply than the color of her eyes. When a friend tries to tell her about the thing that happens in a close place, she says no, saying she does not care about things she does not already know. It seems, then, that a very good idea of what you know and not saying too much are not enough for not being full of you about thinking. What else is needed is a way to look at your own edge – a feeling that they are reasons to keep looking or to listen to people who know more than you.
definition: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.
counterexample: A distinguished mathematician knows precisely the boundaries of her expertise, never overstates her competence, and is punctilious in acknowledging errors and crediting collaborators. Yet she regards her own limitations with serene indifference rather than with any sense that they call for further learning or deference to those who know more. When confronted with a question outside her expertise, she simply declares it uninteresting. She satisfies all three conditions yet displays a complacency that seems antithetical to genuine intellectual humility.
missing_condition: The analysis omits the requirement that the agent regard the limits of them knowledge as reasons for further inquiry or appropriate deference, rather than treating them as matters of indifference.

question: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
expected_answer: Someone could say that a talk is right when it has first ideas and a last idea, when the last idea comes from the first ideas by a known set of ways, and when each part in the step by step making is allowed by a clear way that is said. This idea has a good point of being clear and of and being right with hard ways of new right and wrong ideas. But a case that shows it is not enough comes when we think about the past of right and wrong thinking. The talk that says because all people can die and because the old one who helps people learn is a person then the old one who helps people learn can die was known as right by an old one many years before the time of the one we call the one who helps people learn. Every later one who thinks has agreed. Yet at the time of the first saying there was no known set of ways with clear ways that are said from which the last idea could be got in the way the saying says it needs. The old one old way was the first try to write down such right and wrong thinking, and before that writing the talk being right was felt by a clear feeling of its form. Someone could see that the talk was not less right before the known set of ways was made than after. It seems that the saying, by saying being right is the same as being able to get in a known set of ways, makes a bad about a way of showing being right for the first part of being right itself.
definition: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
counterexample: Consider the argument: all men are mortal; Socrates is a man; therefore, Socrates is mortal. This argument was recognised as valid by Aristotle and by every subsequent logician. Yet when it was first formulated, there existed no recognised formal system with explicitly stated rules of inference from which the conclusion could be derived step by step. The argument's validity was grasped through the intuitive apprehension of its logical structure, not through its conformity to a formal system. Conditions (ii) and (iii) were not satisfied, yet the argument was no less valid for that.
missing_condition: The definition conflates validity with formal derivability, failing to recognise that an argument may be valid in virtue of its logical structure even when no formal system has been devised to codify that structure.

question: counterexample to: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features of the environment, (ii) the content of this experience determines S's motor response, and (iii) the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the perceptual experience.
expected_answer:
Think about these cases of seeing leading to moving. When a person reaches out to take a cup, the seeing of where the cup is makes the hand go to the right place. When a person steps over the raised part of the road, the seeing of how high that part is makes the leg go over it. When a person catches a ball, the seeing of where the ball is going makes the hands go to the right place. In each case, the person has a mind feeling of the parts of the world that matter, that feeling makes the move, and how right the move is comes from how right the feeling is. From these cases one could say: seeing makes moving only when the person has a mind feeling of the parts that matter, that feeling makes the move, and how right the move is comes from how right the feeling is.

This idea, however, is met by a strong brain break. Think about a person who cannot know the shape and way of things she looks at. She cannot say if a hole goes up and down or left and right; she cannot tell or show the things she sees. Yet when she is asked to put a piece of paper through the hole, she puts it in the right way easy. When she is asked to take a thing, her hand opens the right space for the thing big or small. Her moves are led by what she sees with very right, but she has no mind feeling of the parts that matter. The first part does not happen at all. The earlier cases used people who could see and also feel the things, and the saying made the mind feeling a must. The brain case shows that the join is maybe not always needed: seeing can lead to moving through ways that go around the mind feeling.
definition: Perception guides action if and only if (i) S has a conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features of the environment, (ii) the content of this experience determines S's motor response, and (iii) the accuracy of the action depends on the accuracy of the perceptual experience.
counterexample: Consider a patient with visual-form agnosia (damage to the ventral visual stream) who cannot consciously recognise objects or report their orientation, yet who can accurately reach out and grasp them with the appropriate hand aperture. She has no conscious perceptual experience of the object's shape — she cannot describe it or draw it. Yet her motor response is accurately determined by visual information, and the accuracy of her action depends on the accuracy of this visual processing. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are met, but condition (i) is not: there is no conscious perceptual experience of the relevant features. Yet perception — in the form of unconscious visuomotor processing — guides her action with remarkable precision.

question: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
expected_answer:
Think about three ideas that try to show that a god is real. The first idea looks at the whole world and says there must be a first cause. The second idea looks at the way living things seem to be made with a plan and says there must be a person who makes. The third idea looks at the fact that right and wrong seem the same for everyone and says there must be a higher right place. Each idea starts with looking at the world – its being there, its order, its right and wrong – and ends with the thought that a god is real. From these ideas one could make a strong god idea: it starts with clear looking points, the guess of a god cause is the only idea that makes sense, and the end point says what the god is like.

But this way of calling a god idea, taken from ideas that start with looking at the world, is too small. Think about a different idea that starts not with looking but with the thought of a god as a thing that nothing bigger can be thought of. A man in old time said that this thing must be real in the world as well as in the mind, because a thing that is only in the mind is not the most big thing that can be thought of. This idea works only with thinking, not with looking. The first step does not work. Yet many people who think a lot have taken this idea as real, like a man who wrote a book about it, and later people who changed it. The other ideas were all from looking at the world; the way we called a god idea used their looking part as a must, when this only thought idea shows that a only thought road to a god can be and still kept.
definition: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
counterexample: The ontological argument proceeds not from empirical premises but from the concept of a maximally great being. Its premises are a priori, not empirical. The first condition fails entirely, yet the ontological argument has been defended by major philosophers from Anselm to Plantinga and is regarded by many as a serious contribution to natural theology.

question: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
expected_answer:
Think about three places that are like cases of a space. The inside of a room is a space: it has walls, a floor, and a top; it can hold things – a table, a chair, a light; and you can say where any thing is inside it by looking at the walls and the corners. A field is a space: it has side lines and end lines; people who play and a ball move inside it; and you can say where they are by using numbers that you count from the side lines. The inside of a box is a space: it has six sides; you can put things inside it; and you can give a place for a thing by using three directions. From these cases you can make a simple thought: a place is a space only when it has walls or sides, it can hold things, and you can tell where things are by using numbers.

But you also have to think about the open air above a flat sand. A person who flies a big flying thing starts from a small flat ground and goes to the west over a big flat sand that goes to the side all around. Above him the air goes up – light blue, then dark blue, then black high air. He is in a space; people would say it is. He moves in the space, changes where he is in it, and where he is can be said – by numbers that tell where – with as much close as the place of a chair in a room. Things are in this space: birds fly in it, clouds move in it, the big flying thing makes a line in it. Yet this space has no walls. There are no walls, no sides, no lines that mark an end. The sand goes to the side all around, and the air goes up with no top or wall to mark its end. You can not point to a wall and say “here the space ends.” The first part does not work. The first cases were all made by people with walls – rooms, fields, boxes – with clear walls, and the thought said walls are needed for a space. The open air shows that a place can be a space in the most full way – it can hold things, let things move, and you can tell where things are by numbers – even if it has no walls at all. The thought got the parts wrong.
definition: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
counterexample: Consider the open sky above a flat desert, stretching in every direction to the horizon and upward without apparent limit. A pilot flying across this expanse occupies a space — the space above the desert — and his position within it can be described, at least approximately, by coordinates. Objects exist within it: birds, clouds, the aeroplane itself. Yet this space is not bounded in any ordinary sense. The sky does not have borders; it extends continuously in every horizontal direction and fades upward into the atmosphere and beyond. There is no wall, no fence, no line of demarcation that marks where the space ends and something else begins. The first condition fails, yet the sky above the desert is a space — a region within which objects have positions and through which they can move.

question: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
expected_answer:
Someone could say that a group that makes laws for a place is the top group only if it has the final say inside a set land, keeps control of the use of force inside that land, and is said to be the top group by other groups that are also top.  

The first part shows the inside side of being the top: inside its land, the word of the top group is final.  

The second part makes sure that the top group has the ways to make its word be followed.  

The third part adds an outside side: being the top is not just an inside thing but also needs people in many places to say it is true.  

All together, the parts show a group that makes laws for a place that is the most inside, can keep safe itself, and is said to be the top by people outside.  

But someone could see that the part about people saying it is the top brings a strange circle and a hard outside side.  

Think about the place that many people talk about.  

That place has a working group that makes laws and has the final say inside its land.  

It has a group of soldiers and a group of people who keep order that keep control of the use of force inside its edges.  

It makes its own outside plan, makes its own money, and runs its own laws.  

By the first and second parts, that place looks like it is the top.  

But many other places do not say it is the top, mostly because they want to stay nice with the big country that says it owns the land.  

If the law is used exactly, that place is not the top — not because it does not have any real part of being the top, but because some outside groups think it is easier to say it is not.  

The law, it seems, has put the idea of being the top in part in the hands of outside people whose thoughts may show a hard choice instead of any look at how the group really works.
definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
counterexample: Consider Taiwan. Taiwan exercises supreme authority within its territory — it has its own government, legislature, judiciary, and constitution. It controls the use of force within its borders through its own military and police. But a significant number of sovereign states do not recognise Taiwan as a sovereign entity, owing to the political position of the People's Republic of China. Conditions (i) and (ii) are met, but condition (iii) is only partially met. The definition implies that Taiwan's sovereignty depends upon the recognition of other states — that a political entity's sovereignty can be conferred or withheld by external parties whose decisions may be driven by political expediency rather than by any assessment of the entity's actual governance.
missing_condition: The definition makes sovereignty partly dependent on external recognition, but the case reveals that a political entity may exercise all the substantive attributes of sovereignty while being denied recognition for reasons unrelated to its actual governance. A condition grounding sovereignty in the entity's effective exercise of authority rather than in the opinions of other states is needed.
