question: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
expected_answer: One might say that a word has meaning when it has a picture in the mind, can be used to talk, and is part of the words that many people use. This picture thought has been around a long time, and it says that good words are joined to something in the mind. But a case that shows this thought is not enough can be taken from the words we use about right and good in people life. Think about the word we call just. A person who talks uses this word with clear feeling, talks a lot about that thought, and talks well about things of the same way people act with each other, the thought that people get what they have done, the thought that people have what they are allowed to have, and the thought that people must do what they should. The word is part of the words that many many people use. All three parts are there. But it is not clear that the person who talks has any set picture in the mind for that thought. What would that picture look like? Maybe a person who can not see because something covers their eyes holding two sides, but that is a picture that many people use not a picture of what the thought really is. The meaning of that thought is not in any picture in the mind but in the word many other thoughts – the same way people act with each other, the thought that people get what they have done, the thought that people have what they are allowed to have, the thought that people must do what they should – and in the way it is used right in talk among many people about right and good. It seems that the thing that says a word must have a picture to be a good word leaves out or gets the meaning of thoughts that are not about pictures but about how they work in talk.
definition: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
counterexample: Consider the word 'justice.' A speaker uses this word fluently in conversation, communicates successfully with others about matters of fairness and rights, and the word occurs in English, a natural language spoken by a vast community. All three conditions are met. Yet it is doubtful that the speaker, or indeed any speaker, associates a single determinate mental image with 'justice.' The word is meaningful without being imagistic; its meaning resides in its role in discourse about norms, institutions, and obligations, not in any picture before the mind's eye.
missing_condition: The definition fails to allow for meaning that is constituted by a word's inferential or functional role within a language — its systematic connections to other words, its conditions of correct application, and its place in patterns of reasoning — rather than requiring an accompanying mental image, which many meaningful words lack.

question: counterexample to: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, and (iii) f is surjective.
expected_answer:
In small cases that we use to think about size, the fact that we can make a one to one pair between two groups is the same as the thought that not the first or the second group is a part that is not the whole of the other.  

When we put together five red foods with five foods that are a different color, we see not only that a pair is there but also that not the first or the second group has the other inside it.  

When we take paper pieces from two piles at the same time and use up both piles, what happens fits the simple idea that a whole is always bigger than any part that is not the whole.  

This idea asks us to make a big idea: two groups have the same size when we can pair each thing in one group with one thing in the other group.  

But a case that shows this idea is not enough comes from groups that have no end.  

Think about the numbers we use to count and the numbers that are two times bigger. The idea that changes each counting number to the number that is two times bigger is clearly a pair – each thing in the first group goes to a different thing in the second group, and every thing in the second group gets a thing from the first. By this idea, the two groups have the same size.  

Yet the numbers that are two times bigger are a part that is not the whole of the counting numbers: every number that is two times bigger is a counting number, but not every counting number is two times bigger. The idea then says the part has the same size as the whole – a thought that would be not right in a small case.  

One can see that what the small cases have in shared, and what the no end case breaks, is the idea that same size works together with the idea that a part is not as big as the whole. The idea, by not thinking about this idea and using only the pair, moves the idea of same size into a place where it gives what happens that, even if good for counting, do not fit the thoughts we first had.
definition: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, and (iii) f is surjective.
counterexample: Consider the set of natural numbers N = {0, 1, 2, ...} and the set of even natural numbers E = {0, 2, 4, ...}. The function f(n) = 2n is a bijection: it is injective and surjective. By the definition, N and E have the same cardinality. Yet E is a proper subset of N — every even number is a natural number, but not every natural number is even. The definition declares the part equal in size to the whole, violating the principle, unquestioned in the finite case, that a proper part is always smaller.
